error.signature: Datetime comparison produces silently wrong results when mixing timezone-aware and naive datetimes

verdict.summary: Python 3 raises TypeError when comparing aware and naive datetimes. But many ORMs and serializers silently strip timezone info, producing naive datetimes that compare incorrectly. A UTC datetime and a local datetime can appear equal when they're hours apart.

dead_ends.action: Compare datetimes without checking timezone awareness
dead_ends.why_fails: A naive datetime(2024,1,1,9,0) and an aware datetime(2024,1,1,9,0,tzinfo=UTC) may represent different instants. Python raises TypeError, but some frameworks auto-coerce.

dead_ends.action: Use datetime.now() (naive) and assume it's UTC
dead_ends.why_fails: datetime.now() returns local time as naive datetime. datetime.utcnow() returns UTC as naive. Neither has timezone info, so comparisons with aware datetimes fail or are wrong.

workarounds.how: from datetime import datetime, timezone; now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)  # NOT datetime.utcnow()

workarounds.action: Normalize all datetimes to UTC before comparison or storage
workarounds.how: stored_dt = aware_dt.astimezone(timezone.utc)  # store UTC, convert to local only for display

workarounds.action: Use pendulum or arrow library for safer datetime handling
workarounds.how: import pendulum; now = pendulum.now('UTC')  # always timezone-aware, easy conversion
